coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of B, N-co-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the selective detection of hydrogen peroxide and ascorbic acid in food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Junli
Xiang, Guoqiang
He, Lijun
Jiang, Xiuming
Zhao, Renyong
coredata.dc:description: B, N-co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) were prepared using hydrothermal method with 3-amino phenylboronic acid (3-APBA) as a precursor, yielding 82.5 %. The as-prepared B, N-CDs demonstrated highly stable fluorescence emission with a fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of 51.4 % under various experimental conditions including 4 M NaCl solution, 120 days of storage, and solution temperature of 100 °C. Based on the boronate affinity between boric acid groups on the surface of B, N-CDs, and the cis-diol structure of ascorbic acid (AA), a novel fluorescence assay for AA was established with a detection limit of 41 nM. Additionally, based on the redox reaction between hydroperoxide (H2O2) and boric acid groups on B, N-CDs, a fluorescence assay for H2O2 with a detection limit of 4.5 nM was developed. The proposed fluorescent assay methods were successfully applied for the determination of AA and H2O2 traces in food samples, and the spiking recoveries were in the range of 82.5 % −115.75 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B
N-co-doped carbon dots
High product yield
Hydrogen peroxide
Ascorbic acid
Food samples
Boronate affinity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing procedure for AA and H2O2
Fluorescence sensing of AA using B, N-CDs
Fluorescence sensing of H2O2 by B, N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images, (B) FT-IR spectra of B, N-CDs, (C) High-resolution C1s peak, (D) High-resolution B1s peak, (E) High-resolution N1s peak and (F) High-resolution O1s peak of B, N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of B, N-CDs, (B) Fluorescence spectra of B, N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths, (C) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs, (D) Fluorescent stability of B, N-CDs in high-salt solutions, (E) Long-term stability of B, N-CDs, (F) Thermal stability of B, N-CDs.
Effects of metal ion (A) interference and (B) organic small molecules on the detection of AA.
Effects of (A) metal ion interference and (B) organic small molecules on the detection of H2O2.
Schematic diagram of synthetic strategy for B, N-CDs and detection principle for H2O2 and AA.
Recovery test of AA in real samples using the methods.
Detected H2O2 contents in various samples.

row.entry:
Glutinous rice shoots
Jellyfish
Squid
konjac
Almond

head.ce:title: Synthesis of B, N-co-doped carbon dots with high quantum yield for the selective detection of hydrogen peroxide and ascorbic acid in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety and Nutrition, Zhengzhou 450001 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety and Nutrition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety and Nutrition, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: B, N-co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) were prepared using hydrothermal method with 3-amino phenylboronic acid (3-APBA) as a precursor, yielding 82.5 %. The as-prepared B, N-CDs demonstrated highly stable fluorescence emission with a fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of 51.4 % under various experimental conditions including 4 M NaCl solution, 120 days of storage, and solution temperature of 100 °C. Based on the boronate affinity between boric acid groups on the surface of B, N-CDs, and the cis-diol structure of ascorbic acid (AA), a novel fluorescence assay for AA was established with a detection limit of 41 nM. Additionally, based on the redox reaction between hydroperoxide (H2O2) and boric acid groups on B, N-CDs, a fluorescence assay for H2O2 with a detection limit of 4.5 nM was developed. The proposed fluorescent assay methods were successfully applied for the determination of AA and H2O2 traces in food samples, and the spiking recoveries were in the range of 82.5 % −115.75 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), a water-soluble vitamin (vitamin C), plays a crucial role in maintaining healthy physiological functions due to its excellent properties [1,2]. AA deficiency can lead to convulsions, neurological diseases, and even cancer [3]. Based on the consideration of maintaining near-maximum neutrophil concentration and minimum urinary AA excretion, the Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) is set at 75 mg per day for women and 90 mg per day for men. Since AA cannot be synthesized in human body, vegetables and fruits, which contain high levels of AA are the most important source of AA for daily intake [4]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is commonly used as a disinfectant and bleaching agent in the food industry. It is widely employed to prevent microbial proliferation in various types of food processing, including bacon, fish, soybeans, noodles, and freshly cut fruits [5–7]. However, H2O2 entering the human body may accelerate aging, and cause serious disease, including cancer [8]. Therefore, both the US Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA) and China Food Sanitation law specify that food products must not retain H2O2 before they are marketed [9]. Consequently, developing sensitive and rapid methods for AA and H2O2 detection in food samples is widely required.

Analytical methods using various techniques, such as electroanalytical methods [10,11], colorimetric [12,13], fluorescent [14–17], chemiluminescent and electrochemiluminescence methods [18,19] have been developed to date for the detection of AA and H2O2 in foodstuff. Most of these methods are based on peroxidase reaction or the reduction properties of AA. However, some limitations include enzyme activity or stability, interference, and harsh reaction conditions. Therefore, it would be advantageous to expand the repertoire of methods by exploiting novel detection mechanisms.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention as emerging carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials. Due to their properties such as luminescent properties, adjustable fluorescence emission wavelength, ease of surface modification, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, CDs have been widely applied for the fluorescence sensing of organic small molecules, biomolecules and metal ions [20–22]. In the synthesis of CDs, enhancing the FQY has been a challenge. It has been reported that heteroatom doping is an effective strategy to enhance the FQY of carbon dots, specifically the co-doping of multiple heteroatoms [23–26].

Boron is the left-neighbor of carbon in the periodic table of elements. Similar to sulfur and nitrogen, boron also can be covalently doped into CDs as an effective dopant, which alters the electronic and optical properties of CDs. Therefore, boron is an obvious choice for doping CDs. Boronic acid (BA) can bind reversibly with cis-1,2- or cis-1,3-diols, forming a five- or six-membered cyclic ring. Boron-doped CDs (B-CDs) often present high affinity for sugars, vitamins, and coenzymes [27–29]. However, improving the selectivity of B-CDs to single diol compounds has been a challenge for boron affinity analysis systems. Research on novel boron affinity mechanism can enhance the analytical capabilities of B-CDs.

In this study, highly luminescent B, N-CDs with excellent luminescence stability were prepared using one-step hydrothermal method with 3-amino phenylboronic acid (3-APBA) as a single precursor. The B, N-CDs obtained exhibited a UV fluorescence emission peak at 370 nm with an FQY of 51.4 %. At a temperature of 70 °C, H2O2 reacted with boric acid present on the surface of B, N-CDs, resulting in significant fluorescence quenching of B, N-CDs. Based on this, a novel fluorescence assay method for the analysis of H2O2 residues in food was developed. At 80 °C, AA and boric acid on the surface of B, N-CDs formed boronate ester, which significantly quenched the fluorescence of B, N-CDs. A novel fluorescence assay for the determination of AA in fruits and vegetables was established based on boron affinity. Both assays were successfully applied for the detection of H2O2 and AA in food samples, yielding satisfactory results (Scheme 1 ).

3-APBA was procured from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). H2O2, AA, and B vitamins (including vitamin B1 (VB1), vitamin B3 (VB3), vitamin B5 (VB5), and vitamin B12 (VB12)) were procured from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Se6+, Pb2+, Cr3+ and Mg2+), amino acids (lysine, glutamate, phenylalanine, and glutathione), sugars (sucrose, lactose, glucose, and fructose), oxalic acid and sodium chloride were procured from Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (combining the same volume of H3BO3, H3PO4, and CH3COOH, the required pH was adjusted with NaOH solution) was applied to control pH of testing solutions. All chemicals and reagents were in analytical reagent grade, and used without any further purification. All solutions were prepared in ultrapure water (18 MΩ·cm).

The morphology, elemental composition, and surface groups were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 plus, Japan), an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, USA) and FT-IR spectrometer (WQF-530, Beijing Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument, China), respectively. The optical properties of B, N-CDs were investigated using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometers (F-6000, Shimadzu, Japan and FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments, France).

B, N-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal procedure. Approximately, 0.2 g of 3-APBA was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and the homogeneous solution obtained was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber, heated to 180 °C, and reacted for 10 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the solution obtained was filtered to remove the insoluble solids. Activated carbon powder (1 g) was added to the filtrate and stirred for 2 h, causing the solution color to change from brownish yellow to light yellow. The activated carbon powder was removed using centrifugation, and the pale-yellow B, N-CDs powder (0.165 g) was obtained using freeze-drying. The product yield for B, N-CDs was calculated to be 82.5 %, higher than that for most reported B, N-CDs [30,31]. The resulting B, N-CDs powder was re-dispersed in ultrapure water (16.5 mg·L−1) for subsequent characterization.

Natural food samples, such as broccoli, bitter gourd, cucumber, red pepper, green pepper, cauliflower, pear, watermelon, cherry tomato, lemon, glutinous rice shoots, jellyfish, squid, konjac, and almond were purchased from a local supermarket (Zhengzhou, China). Dandelion, plantain, and hosta were collected from the campus of Henan University of Technology (Zhengzhou, China).

For the fluorescence sensing of AA, vegetables (broccoli, bitter melon, cucumber, red pepper, green pepper, and cauliflower), fruits (pear, watermelon, cherry tomato, and lemon), and wild vegetables (dandelion, plantain, and jade) were selected as representative samples. The AA content in fruit samples was extracted using an oxalic acid solution [17]. Approximately 10 g of the edible part of the samples were randomly taken and mixed with 10 mL of 0.2 g·L−1 oxalic acid. The food samples were crushed and filtered. The extract was finally filtered using 0.45 μm water filter membrane and immediately analyzed.

For the determination of H2O2 residue in food, simulated food samples were prepared using the H2O2 treatment procedure for water-soaked foodstuffs as reference. Five food samples namely, glutinous rice buds, jellyfish, squid, konjac, and almond were soaked in H2O2 solution (v/v, 0.5 %) for half an hour, and dried naturally. For the determination of H2O2 residue in food, approximately 10 g of the treated sample was added to 500 mL of deionized water and stirred for 1 min, and the filtrate was collected after filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing procedure for AA and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: AA and H2O2 were detected through a similar fluorescence sensing procedure. About 0.5 mL of B, N-CDs solution (16.5 mg·L−1), 1 mL of Briton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 9, 10 mM) and a certain amount of AA (or H2O2) standard solution (or the sample solution) were subsequently added into a calibrated tube. Then the mixture was diluted to the required volume with deionized water. After incubating at 80 °C for 30 min (For H2O2 sensing, 70 °C for 10 min), the fluorescence intensities (λ = 370 nm) of the test solutions were measured at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. All samples for fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate. The calculated log (I0/I) was used for spectrometric quantification, where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities with and without AA (or H2O2) in the test solutions, respectively.

The morphological structure of B, N-CDs characterized by TEM is displayed in Fig. 1 A. The TEM image demonstrated that the B, N-CDs are well-dispersed, with an average size of 6 nm (Fig. S1A, illustrated in supporting information). The discernible lattice fringes (lattice spacing of 0.203 nm) for B, N-CDs were observed in Fig. S1B, which is attributed to the sp2 (1120) graphite crystal phase of graphene [32].

The functional groups on the surface of B, N-CDs were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). In the FT-IR spectrum obtained, the absorption peaks at 3500 cm−1 and 3174 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H, respectively. The absorption peaks at 3043 cm−1, 1693 cm−1, 1511 cm−1, and 760 cm−1 are characteristic peaks of the benzene ring. The absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of B-O. These results indicated that B, N-CDs possess characteristic functional groups such as amino, borate, and phenyl.

The elemental composition and valence state of B, N-CDs were confirmed using XPS. Its scanning spectrum indicated that B, N-CDs are primarily composed of elements, such as C, O, N, and B, with atomic ratios of 55.4 %, 24.7 %, 7.8 %, and 12.1 % (Fig. S2), respectively. XPS analysis of the C1s spectrum demonstrated three peaks at 284.24, 284.81 and 285.96 eV, attributed to C-B, C=C and C-N bonds, respectively (Fig. 1C). The B1s spectrum indicated the boron signals of B-C bond at 191.44 eV and B-O bond at 192.78 eV [33] (Fig. 1D). In the high-resolution N1s spectrum, three peaks at 399.49, 400.5 and 401.67 eV could be assigned to C-N-C, N-C and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. 1E). The O1s spectrum indicated two peaks at 532.29 and 533.04 eV, which were attributed to O-B and O-H bonds [34] (Fig. 1F). These results indicate that the elemental composition and functional group information of B, N-CDs obtained using XPS were consistent with the results obtained from FT-IR spectrum.

Ultraviolet absorption curve and fluorescence spectra were recorded to investigate the optical properties of B, N-CDs. Fig. 2 A shows that B, N-CDs exhibit two typical absorption peaks at 290 nm and 230 nm, attributed to the n-π* transition of C=C-N and the π-π* transition of C=C bond [35], respectively. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence spectra of B, N-CDs indicate that the fluorescence emission is not dependent on excitation wavelength, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 310 nm and 373 nm, respectively. The FQY of B, N-CDs was measured to be 51.4 % in solution using quinine sulfate as reference standard. Compared to recently reported B-CDs fluorescent probes, the FQY has significantly increased [36–39]. This increase in FQY can be attributed to the higher B doping amount with a B mass content of about 10.1 %.

The luminescence stability of B, N-CDs was evaluated by investigating the effect of pH, salt concentration, temperature, and storage duration on the fluorescence properties of B, N-CDs Fig. 2C shows that the fluorescence properties of B, N-CDs are pH-sensitive, with the fluorescence intensity decreasing sharply in strongly acidic or alkaline media. This is due to the binding of –NH2 on the surface of B, N-CDs to H+ ion in strong acidic conditions, and the binding of −B(OH)2 to OH− ion in strong alkaline conditions.

The effect of salt concentration, temperature, and storage duration on the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs were also studied. The results indicated that B, N-CDs possess excellent fluorescence stability. The fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs remained unchanged in 4.0 M NaCl solution (Fig. 2D). The fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs in solution remained constant after storage for 120 days, (Fig. 2E). The fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs decreased by 10 % at a solution temperature of 100 °C compared to room temperature (Fig. 2F). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs remained constant after heating at 30 °C and 70 °C for 5 h (Fig. S3). Unlike other CDs reported in the literature, which were temperature sensitive, and exhibited significant decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing solution temperature [40–42], some recently reported CDs demonstrated temperature stability [43–48]. However, the temperature for stable fluorescence of these CDs does not exceed 70 °C. Unfortunately, the underlying mechanism for temperature stability of B, N-CDs still needs further study [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of AA using B, N-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The objective of designing B, N-CDs was to utilize boronate affinity for the development of highly selective fluorescence analysis methods. The AA molecule has a cis-diol structure that forms borate esters with boric acid on the surface of B, N-CDs under alkaline conditions. It was observed that the fluorescence of B, N-CDs significantly decreased after the addition of AA under thermal conditions. The fluorescence lifetime of B, N-CDs, UV absorption spectra of the mixture, and the Stern-Volmer equation were used to confirm the interaction mechanism between AA and B, N-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of B, N-CDs before and after mixing with AA was observed to be constant (Fig. S4A), indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism between B, N-CDs, and AA is static. The UV absorption spectrum of the system after mixing indicated that the B, N-CDs-AA complex was formed in the mixture, which further confirmed the static quenching mechanism between B, N-CDs, and AA (Fig. S4B).

The primary parameters influencing the fluorescence response of B, N-CDs to AA include pH, reaction temperature, and incubation time. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (FQE) of B, N-CDs using AA was applied as an evaluation index, and all three parameters were optimized using a single-factor optimization experiment. As illustrated in Fig. S5A, S5B, and S5C, the optimal experimental conditions for AA fluorescence sensing were selected as follows: pH 9, reaction temperature 80 °C, and incubation time 30 min. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence response of B, N-CDs to AA was recorded (Fig. S6A), and a linear relationship between the calculated lg(I0/I) and AA concentrations (0.05 µM to 5 µM) was achieved (Fig. S6B). The calibration equation was lg(I0/I) = 0.12248C + 0.01548 (R2 = 0.9952) with a calculated detection limit (3σ) of 41 nmol·L−1, and a relative deviation (RSD) of 0.6 % (C = 1 µM, n = 11). Compared to other reported CDs-based fluorescence assays for AA, this method possesses lower limits of detection and quantification within a wider linear response range (Table S1).

To evaluate the selectivity of the AA sensing system, the influence of the potential interfering compounds, including metal ions, amino acids, sugars, and B-group vitamins(VB), on the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs was investigated. The sensing system containing AA (1.0 µM) and potential interfering compounds was tested according to the recommended procedures. As evident from Fig. 3 A, metal ions (1 μg·mL−1, Fe2+:0.2 μ g·mL−1; Fe3+:0.1 μ g·mL−1) did not influence the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs in the absence or presence of AA. The selectivity of AA sensing was also investigated using amino acids, sugars, and B vitamins (Fig. 3B). The results indicated that amino acids, sugars, and B-group vitamins (100 µM) did not significantly interfere with AA sensing. The tolerance concentrations for glucose, fructose, VB1, and VB12 were observed to be 10 µM, as sugar molecules can also have an affinity for boron. From the above, the B, N-CDs emerged as a highly selective fluorescent probe for AA in food samples.

The feasibility of the boronate affinity-based fluorescent assay was validated by analyzing various food samples. The analytical results and the recoveries for the spiked samples are tabulated in Table 1 . It was observed that the recoveries for the spiked samples ranged from 88.50 % to 115.20 % with the RSD not exceeding 5.85 %. This was further confirmed by assessing the accuracy of the developed fluorescent assay method. The same samples were analyzed using the China national standard method (GB5009.86–2016, 2,6-dichloro-indophenol titration method), and the consistency of the analytical results between the two methods was confirmed using the paired t-test (Table S2). The corresponding t values for the three food samples (3.0285, 2.6774, and 2.9751) were lower than t0.05,2 = 4.3 (t-test with 95 % confidence level), indicating no statistical significance between the two methods. This demonstrated that fluorescent assay possesses high accuracy, sensitivity, and precision. Furthermore, various vegetable samples were also selected to validate the feasibility of this method, and the analytical results are presented in Table S3, indicating the significant potential of this method for the determination of AA in food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of H2O2 by B, N-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the boronate affinity reaction, the redox reaction between phenylboronic group and H2O2 was also studied, which was utilized for the fluorescence assay of H2O2 [49,50]. Under alkaline conditions, H2O2 can remove the boric acid group from the phenylboronic group to form a phen group (Scheme 1). The effect of H2O2 on the fluorescence of B, N-CDs was investigated. It indicated that the addition of H2O2 resulted in a significant fluorescence quenching on B, N-CDs under thermal conditions.

The fluorescent assay for H2O2 was developed based on B, N-CDs, with the parameters affecting the redox reaction, including pH, reaction temperature, and incubation time, optimized using FQE as the evaluation index. As shown in Fig. S7A, the FQE was higher under alkaline conditions compared to acidic conditions, with the maximum FQE achieved at pH = 9. Fig. S7B illustrates that higher FQE was obtained at higher reaction temperatures. However, when the reaction temperature exceeded 70 °C, the offline heating mode influenced fluorescence detection due to the air-cooling effect, impacting the precision. Therefore, the optimal reaction temperature of 70 °C was selected. Fig. S7C indicates that 10 min is sufficient to achieve a higher FQE.

Under optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence response of B, N-CDs to H2O2 within the concentration range of 0.01 µM to 10 µM was monitored (Fig. S8A). A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree lg(I0/I) and H2O2 concentrations was observed with a linear equation of lg(I0/I) = 0.08322C-0.01039(R2 = 0.99208) (Fig. S8B). The detection limit (3σ) was calculated as 4.5 nmol·L−1, and RSD was 0.3 % (C = 5 µM, n = 11). In comparison to other CDs-based fluorescence assay methods reported in the literature, this method exhibited significantly higher sensitivity and was simpler and faster than methods based on electrochemical and peroxidase mimic principles (Table S4).

The selectivity of fluorescent response of B, N-CDs to H2O2 was evaluated, considering metal ions, sugars, amino acids, and B-group vitamins as typical interferences in food samples. The fluorescence intensities of B, N-CDs were recorded for metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Se6+, Pb2+, Cr3+ and Mg2+), sugars (sucrose, lactose, glucose, and fructose), amino acids (glutamate, phenylalanine, and glutathione) and B-group vitamins (VB1, VB12, VB3, and VB5) in the absence and presence of H2O2. The results are presented in Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B. At 1 μM H2O2, metal ions (1 μg·mL−1) had no significant influence on the fluorescence sensing. Sugars, amino acids, and B vitamins (all VB at 100 μM, excluding VB1 and VB12 at 10 μM) exhibited significant interference on the fluorescence sensing of H2O2. Therefore, it can be inferred that the H2O2 fluorescence sensing method based on B, N-CDs exhibits high selectivity.

The feasibility of the B, N-CDs-based fluorescence assay was validated by analyzing food samples treated with H2O2 solution following the sample pretreatment procedure specified in section 2.4. The H2O2 −treated food samples were tested using the developed fluorescence method. The analytical results and spiked recoveries are presented in Table 2 . The spiked recoveries ranged from 82.50 to 115.75 %, indicating the fluorescence assay exhibits high accuracy, sensitivity, and precision. Therefore, this method has great potential for monitoring H2O2 in food samples.

In summary, B, N-CDs were successfully prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method with 3-APBA as a single precursor, yielding 82.5 %. The obtained B, N-CDs exhibited unique high fluorescence stability, and the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs solution was not significantly decreased at 100 °C compared to that at room temperature. Based on the affinity of the phenylboronic group on the surface of B, N-CDs with AA and H2O2, novel fluorescence assays for AA and H2O2 was constructed with detection limits of 41 nM (AA) and 4.5 nM (H2O2). Based on high sensitivity and selectivity, these assays have been successfully applied to the quantitation of trace AA and H2O2 in fruit and vegetable samples, which indicates the great potential for monitoring of AA and H2O2 in foods.

Junli Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Guoqiang Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Lijun He: Resources, Project administration. Xiuming Jiang: Validation, Project administration. Renyong Zhao: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFF1104600) and Joint Research Fund for science and technology R&D Projects of Henan Province (Grant No. 225200810066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112828.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activity of ascorbic acid
Antioxidants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Agnieszka, S. Elzbieta, Antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activity of ascorbic acid, Antioxidants 11(10) (2022) 1993-1993, https://doi.org/10.3390/antiox11101993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near infrared graphene quantum dots-based two-photon nanoprobe for direct bioimaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. L. Feng, Y. X. Wu, D. L. Zhang, X. X. Hu, J. Zhang, P. Wang, Z. L. Song, X. B. Zhang, W. Tan, Near infrared graphene quantum dots-based two-photon nanoprobe for direct bioimaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells, Anal. Chem. 89(7) (2017) 4077-4084, https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.6b04943.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vitamin C and bone health: evidence from cell, animal and human studies
Curr. Drug Targets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Y. Chin, S. Ima-Nirwana, Vitamin C and bone health: evidence from cell, animal and human studies, Curr. Drug Targets 19(5) (2018) 439-450, https://doi.org/10.2174/1389450116666150907100838.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of highly crystalline nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application in sequential fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xiamin, M. Huasheng, D. Lijuan, H. Jianying, W.
